name: Deucalion
description: Deucalion, son of Prometheus and Pronoia, occupies a significant position in Greek mythology as the progenitor of humankind after the Great Deluge. His story, primarily recounted in Ovid's *Metamorphoses* and Apollodorus' *Bibliotheca*, details his piety and wisdom which led Zeus to spare him from the devastating flood intended to punish the wickedness of humanity. Deucalion, warned by his father Prometheus, constructed a chest with his wife Pyrrha, daughter of Epimetheus and Pandora, and survived the deluge. After the waters receded, Deucalion and Pyrrha consulted the oracle of Themis, who instructed them to cast the bones of their mother behind them. Interpreting this riddle as referring to Gaia, Mother Earth, they cast stones behind them, from which new men and women arose, thus repopulating the world. Deucalion's role highlights themes of divine justice, human resilience, and the cyclical nature of destruction and rebirth in Greek cosmology. He is often viewed as the Greek counterpart to the biblical Noah, though with distinct cultural and theological nuances.
subtitle: The Greek Noah

keyMyths.title: The Great Deluge
keyMyths.description: Zeus, angered by the impiety of mankind, decided to flood the earth and destroy all living beings. Prometheus, foreseeing this catastrophe, warned his son Deucalion to build a chest. Deucalion and his wife, Pyrrha, entered the chest and floated for nine days and nights as the world was consumed by water. This myth emphasizes the importance of piety and divine favor in the face of overwhelming destruction, and sets the stage for the repopulation of the world by Deucalion and Pyrrha.

keyMyths.title: The Oracle of Themis
keyMyths.description: After the flood subsided and Deucalion and Pyrrha were the only two humans left, they sought guidance from the oracle of Themis. The oracle instructed them to cast the bones of their mother behind them. Initially confused, they eventually realized that the oracle meant the stones of the earth, their 'mother.' By casting these stones, Deucalion and Pyrrha gave birth to a new generation of humans. This myth highlights the importance of interpreting divine messages and the power of rebirth and renewal.

keyMyths.title: Rebuilding Humanity
keyMyths.description: The stones cast by Deucalion transformed into men, while the stones cast by Pyrrha transformed into women. This act of repopulation marked the beginning of a new age for humanity, one founded on the resilience and piety of Deucalion and Pyrrha. The myth underscores the potential for humanity to learn from past mistakes and rebuild a better world, emphasizing the importance of moral behavior and respect for the divine. Deucalion's act is seen as a second creation of humanity, different from the original creation by Prometheus.
